revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ito (Staat)]][[en:Itokoku]][[ja:伊都国]][[zh:伊都国]]
{{Expand German}}{{Short description|Historical Japanese State}} {{Use dmy dates|date=January 2024}} [[File:Wajinden diagram.svg|thumb|map illustrating the path from the {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|qid=Q704994|lt=Daifeng commandery|1=Daifang Commandery}} to {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}}, and its distances in the {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}.]] {{nihongo|Ito-koku|伊都国|1=Itokoku}} is one of the countries in {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424|lt=Wa-koku|1=Wakoku}}, which appears in Chinese historical books such as {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}. It is said to be located 500 ri southeast of [[Matsurokoku]], in the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato era|1=Yamato period}}, in the {{ill|Prefecture|en|Prefecture|qid=Q515716|lt=Prefecture|1=Prefecture}} of Ito (Ito no Agata), now {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima, Fukuoka|qid=Q1011145|lt=Itoshima City|1=Itoshima}} and part of {{ill|Nishi-ku, Fukuoka|en|Nishi-ku, Fukuoka|qid=Q1132402|lt=Fukuoka City|1=Nishi-ku, Fukuoka}} (formerly {{ill|Ito-gun|ja|怡土郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ito-gun|1=Ito district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11072672}}).
== Overview == In {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}} it says as follows {{Blockquote|text=Travel 500 miles southeast to Itokoku. The official was called Erskine, and the deputy was called Goblet and Goblet. There are more than 1,000 families and kings, all of whom belong to the Queen. The county envoys are always stationed there}}
("Sanguozhi Wei Shu, Vol. 30, Biography of the East, Japanese (Abbreviated as Wei Shu, Biography of the East)")
The approximate meaning of the original text is: "If you go 500 ri southeast, you will reach Ito-kuni. There are more than 1,000 houses. There were kings for generations.<ref>魏書内で「世有」は魏の世（魏が政権を持っていた時代）のEventsを指しており、通説では王を複数にする為に魏の世内で「代々居た」と訳される。</ref>。
They are all subordinate to the queen state.<ref>この「みな」は爾支、副官は泄謨觚と柄渠觚、千余戸、王、つまり伊都国に居住する者全てを指す。</ref> It is a place where emissaries from {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|qid=Q704994|lt=Daifang Commandery|1=Daifang Commandery}} come and go and stop." The following is a brief description of the area.
{{ill|Weilüe|en|Weilüe|qid=Q868011|lt=Weilüe|1=Weilüe}} says 「500 miles southeast to Idukuni. There are more than 10,000 families. The officials were called Ershi, and the deputies were called Leixi and Shankui. The kings of the kingdom were all under the queen.」
The approximate meaning of the original text is: "Go southeast 500 ri (from Suoro-gun) and you will reach Ito-gun, where there are more than 1,000 houses. The chief magistrate there is called Jiqi, and his lieutenants are called Sesukei and Heikyong. All the kings of the land belong to the queen".
In the "Wei Shihwa Den" and the "Wei Narrative," it is stated that "kings" existed in Itokoku, {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}}, and {{ill|Kununokuni|en|Kununokuni|qid=Q11571391|lt=Gunoku|1=Kununokuni}}, while in the other countries only the names of officials such as directors and deputy directors are mentioned.
Former editor Takeshi Ueno also deciphered "Wei-Shi-Wa-jin-Den" from the perspective of historical criticism and concluded that Himiko's capital was Itsukoku. (Related to the Hirabara site mentioned below)
=== {{Anchor|Ichidaisotsu}}''Ichidaisotsu'': Inspector General === The {{Nihongo|''{{ill|Ichidaisotsu|en|Ichidaisotsu|qid=Q65247767|lt=Ichidaisotsu|1=Ichidaisotsu}}''|一大率||{{lit|great leader}}}} was an official of Yamatai working under Queen [[Himiko]].
The ''{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}'' records that this office was responsible for "自女王國以北", translating roughly to "The Queen's Land and Northward".
A fuller excerpt:
* 特置一大率検察諸國 諸國畏揮之 常治伊都國 於國中有如刺史.
The approximate meaning of the original text is: "The Queen Country has placed a special {{ill|inspector general|en|inspector general|qid=Q2305417|lt=inspector general|1=Inspector general}} in the north. The nations fear this (=the Queen Country) and take care of it (=the Queen Country). A permanent government office (like the {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|qid=Q704994|lt=Daifang County|1=Daifang Commandery}} "reign" of {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|qid=Q320930|lt=Wei|1=Cao Wei}}) was established in Ito Country. It appears to be the same position as that of the stabber in the Guozhong (= Wei)."
The {{nihongo|''Ichidaisotsu''|一大卒||{{lit|great leader}}}} was official position in the [[Itokoku|kingdom of Ito]] in northern Kyushu during the late 3rd century. According to the {{nihongo|''{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}''|魏志倭人伝|Gishi Wajinden|those passages in the 30th volume of the Chinese history chronicle ''{{ill|Records of the Three Kingdoms|en|Records of the Three Kingdoms|qid=Q247318|lt=Records of the Three Kingdoms|1=Records of the Three Kingdoms}}'' that talk about the {{ill|Wajin (ancient people)|en|Wajin (ancient people)|qid=Q10888418|lt=''Wa'' people|1=Wajin (ancient people)}}}}, this office was responsible for investigating and reporting on those regions north of the kingdom of Yamatai. The office was apparently feared and respected by those in Ito. The ''ichidaisotsu'' also served as {{ill|Envoy (title)|en|Envoy (title)|qid=Q11051391|lt=envoy|1=Envoy (title)}} between the queen and the capital city of the Wei kingdom, the {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|qid=Q704994|lt=Daifang Commandery|1=Daifang Commandery}}, and {{ill|Three Kingdoms of Korea|en|Three Kingdoms of Korea|qid=Q165292|lt=the various kingdoms on the Korean peninsula|1=Three Kingdoms of Korea}}. Conversely, when the Daifang Commandery sent envoys, the ''ichidaisotsu'' was responsible for communicating with them and for ensuring that documents and gifts were accurately conveyed to the queen.<ref name="三訂版-2023a">{{Cite web |last=三訂版 |first=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),世界大百科事典 第２版,旺文社日本史事典 |title=一大率(いちだいそつ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%80%E5%A4%A7%E7%8E%87-204144 |access-date=2023-01-24 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
It is not clear who the primary authority behind the establishment of the ''Ichidaisotsu'' was, with theories that it was either the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|qid=Q320930|lt=kingdom of Wei|1=Cao Wei}} or {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}}. One source suggests that the Chinese origin of the ''sotsu'' in the title could suggest that the official may have been sent from the {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|qid=Q704994|lt=Daifang Commandery|1=Daifang Commandery}};<ref name="三訂版-2023a" /> however, the entirety of the title is from Chinese, not just the ''sotsu'' portion.
=== Descriptions in Japanese literature === The former Yido County was located in Itsuki-gata before the Taika Reform. According to [[Nihon Shoki|The Chronicles of Japan]], the founder of Yido County was named Itote, and he returned to Japan when Emperor Chūai {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|qid=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}} conquered Tsukushi. There is an Itote shrine in the Nishi ward of [[:en:Fukuoka City|Fukuoka City]].
According to an anecdote in the {{nihongo|Chikuzen-no-kuni-fudoki|筑前国風土記|Chikuzen Province Fudoki}}, the shrine was praised as "Isoshi" because it welcomed and served the emperor who went to Tsukushi. The name "Isoshi" is said to have been derived from "Ito" (伊覩). It is also said that Gojatote told the emperor that he was a descendant of Amenohiboko (≠ [[Amenohiboko|Amanihiboko no Mikoto]]), who had descended from the heavens to Mount Iro in Goryeo.
== Archaeological Site == It is widely believed that Ito Province existed in the area of the Itoshima Plain centered on Mikumo in Itoshima City. From the latter half of the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} to the end of the Yayoi period, a series of Kofuns (royal tombs) were constructed, including the San-un Minami-shoji and Hirabaru ruins. The Ibara Yarimizo site is said to have a high possibility of being a "Shogun's tomb" in terms of artifacts.
=== Sangunnaminamikoji Ruins === The Sangunnaminamikoji Site (National Historic Site) is a {{ill|Yayoi era tomb system|lt=Yayoi era tomb|ja|弥生時代の墓制|1=Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi era tomb system|13=User:Immanuelle/Funerary practices of the Yayoi period|qq=}} of the middle Yayoi period, and is said to be a '<nowiki/>'''royal tomb'''' with two {{ill|Jar burial|en|Jar burial|qid=Q4244233|lt=jar coffin tombs|1=Jar burial}}.
The coffin tomb No. 1 has 1 {{ill|Bronze Age sword|en|Bronze Age sword|qid=Q3965852|lt=copper sword|1=Bronze Age sword}}, 2 {{ill|Dohoku|ja|銅矛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dohoku|1=dōhoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11650189}}, 1 {{ill|Douka spear|lt=Douka|ja|銅戈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Douka spear|1=dōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11650186}}, 8 or more broken pieces of glass, 3 glass [[Magatama|Kudatama]], 3 pieces of glass {{ill|Kudatama|ja|管玉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudatama|qq=}} 60 or more, {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=copper mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} 31 or more surfaces, gilded copper four-leaf zaguar<ref>A gilded copper wooden coffin ornament, presented by a former Han emperor upon the death of a person who was a "king".</ref> for eight individuals, for example. In addition, 1 {{ill|Tetsuzoku|ja|鉄鏃|1=Iron arrowheads|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzoku|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron arrowheads|qq=}} and 1 small glass bead were excavated.<ref name="#1">「悲劇の金印」原田大六著</ref> Most of the mirrors are of the heavy circle or [[Flower mirror]] style, beginning with "Kiyoseihaku", and the outer rim matching a mirror with an uchigyoka design, which has been handed down at {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Fukuoka)|en|Shōfuku-ji (Fukuoka)|qid=Q657825|lt=Shōfuku-ji Temple|1=Shōfuku-ji (Fukuoka)}} in {{ill|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|en|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|qid=Q1330839|lt=Hakata-ku|1=Hakata-ku, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka City, has been excavated. The diameter of this mirror is 16.4 centimeters.<ref name="mikumoihara">岡崎敬「三雲・井原遺跡とその年代」『魏志倭人伝の考古学』第一書房 2003rd year </ref> The No. 2 jar coffin tomb is located close (15 cm across) to the northwest of the No. 1 jar coffin tomb. Inside the jar coffin, a mirror with an inner flower pattern ([[:en:Variant character band mirror|Sunlight mirror]]) was found in its original position. The bronze mirror is 6.5 cm in diameter and bears the inscription "Seeing the light of the sun, it will be very clear under the heavens. The burial accessories<ref name="#1" /> consisted of 22 bronze mirrors (1 with a nebula design, 4 with [[:en:Variant character band mirror|inscription on inner band mirror]], 1 with a heavy circle design, and 16 sun mirrors), 12 small glass jade beads, 1 hard jade bead, and a glass hanging decoration (measuring less than 1 cm in diameter and dark blue in color). dark blue) were excavated. Many of the bronze mirrors are around 6.5 cm in diameter.<ref name="mikumoihara" /> If Tomb 1 is the "king," Tomb 2 is presumed to be the "queen. Tetsunobu Aoyagi wrote that the tomb was a mound tomb with a fill of more than 1.5 meters. In a survey conducted in the Heisei period, it was found to have a "perforated ditch," and it was identified as a "square perforated ditch tomb," and it is currently being reconstructed as such.
The bronze sword with a handle excavated from the tomb is said to be related to the [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Amenomora no Ken]] enshrined in the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]<ref>原田大六『実在した神話 -発掘された平原弥生古墳-』[[学生社]]出版、1966th year 。</ref>
=== Ihara Yarimizo Ruins === The Ihara Yarimizo Ruins are located in the vicinity of the Migumo Ruins. (According to local legend, the name "Yarimizo" came from the fact that a spear ({{ill|Spear|en|Spear|qid=Q44475|lt=Yari|1=Spear}}) was found in the soil.) According to Aoyagi Tadanobu's book, "Ryuen Kouki Kouki Kou", 21 mirrors were excavated from this site during the {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|qid=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei|1=Tenmei}} period (1781–1788). Rubbings indicate that all of them are square rectangular mirrors with four divine motifs (with edges of flowing cloud, grass leaf, wave, and ninjutsu-style flower patterns). Many of the mirrors are {{ill|Eastern Han|en|Eastern Han|qid=Q1147037|lt=Later Han|1=Eastern Han}} scale and measure 6", dating from the {{ill|Xin dynasty|en|Xin dynasty|qid=Q504769|lt=Xin|1=Xin dynasty}} period of {{ill|Wang Mang|en|Wang Mang|qid=Q7250|lt=Wang 莽|1=Wang Mang}} to the Later Han dynasty. In addition to these mirrors, three bronze vessels in the shape of a ba-shaped bronze, iron swords, and iron swords have been found, but no fine-shaped bronze swords or bronze contradictions have been found. The 1974 ([[:en:Shōwa (1926-1989)|Showa]]49) – 1975 (Showa 50) surveys could not confirm the location of this site. However, it is believed that it was definitely a jar coffin tomb.<ref name="mikumoihara" />。
=== Hirahara Site === The Hirahara Site (a national historic site) is the combined name of a group of five peri-ditch tombs from the late Yayoi Period to the end of the Yayoi Period that exist on the Sone terrace to the west of the San'unnamikoji Site. The ''king's tomb'' in tomb No. 1 may be the '''queen's tomb'''.<ref>「実在した神話」原田大六著</ref> It is said that the tomb is the largest of its kind in Japan. The largest burial item in Japan, 46.5 cm in diameter, was found in Tomb No. 1 (Hirahara Yayoi Kofun) at the Hirahara Site.<ref>漢の尺度で「二尺」となり、その円周は「八咫」となる。この事から「[[八咫鏡]]」の実物ではないか？という説（原田大六の説）ある。</ref> No. [[:en:The large inward-looking flower mirror|Large inward-looking flower mirror]] (inward-looking flower mirror) 4 sides<ref>「平原弥生古墳 大日孁貴の墓 原田大六著</ref>(5 sides)<ref>前原市文化財調査報告書、第七十集、平原遺跡</ref>）、The collection includes 35 bronze mirrors (32 rectangular four-faced mirrors, 2 four-leaf mirrors with inner rows of flowers, and 1 four-faced mirror), 3 glass beads, over 500 round beads, 12 agate balls, many glass balls and small glass beads, 1 iron sword with a ring head, and other items, all of which have been designated {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. The relationship with the [[Yata no Kagami|Yata Kagami]] of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} is under debate.
== See also ==
* {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}} * {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} * {{ill|Sone Sites|en|Sone Sites|qid=Q11515360|lt=Sone Sites|1=Sone Sites}} * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Ruins|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}

* 「柳園古器略考」青柳種信著 * 「原田大六論」{{ill|Dairoku Harada|en|Dairoku Harada|qid=Q5209551|lt=原田大六|1=Dairoku Harada}}著 * 「実在した神話」原田大六著 * 「平原弥生古墳 大日孁貴の墓」原田大六著 * 「悲劇の金印」原田大六著 * 「倭女王 大日孁貴の墓」井手將雪著 * 「前原市文化財調査報告書 第七十集 平原遺跡」
== External links == * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%BC%8A%E9%83%BD%E5%9B%BD-31720 伊都国とは] – {{ill|kotobank|en|kotobank|qid=Q11302260|lt=kotobank|1=Kotobank}} * [http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/soshiki/33/hakubutsukan.html 糸島市伊都国歴史博物館] {{Yamatai}}
[[en:Itokoku]]
<!--東南陸行五百里 至伊都國。官曰爾支 副曰泄謨觚・柄渠觚。有千余戸 丗有王 皆統属女王國。郡使往来常所駐--><!-- 東南五百里 到伊都國。戸万余。置官曰爾支 副曰洩渓・柄渠。其國王皆属女王也 --> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Ito (Staat)]] [[ja:伊都国]] [[zh:伊都国]] {{wikidata link|Q10885162}} [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[Category:History of the Kyushu region]] [[Category:States of the Wajinden]] [[Category:Wajinden]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 100+ untranslated japanese characters]]
